coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence-tunable carbon dots from synergy effect of sulfur doping and water engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Dong
Zhang, Yusheng
Liu, Amin
Zhu, Yuda
Chen, Suping
Wei, Dan
Sun, Jing
Guo, Zhenzhen
Fan, Hongsong
coredata.dc:description: Multiple-color-emissive carbon dots (CDs) is one of the hotspots due to its potential in multi-fields. However, the exploring of efficient synthetic strategies and emission mechanisms still remain challenging. Herein, we report a facile solvothermal route to prepare CDs with tunable photoluminescence (PL) from green to red via a novel PL-tuning strategy of sulfur doping and water engineering. By detailed characterizations and comparison, it is demonstrated that the S doping can significantly increase the content of graphitic nitrogen, resulting in the fluorescence shifting from green to yellow; on the other hand, water engineering is able to yield carboxyl group and enhance surface oxidation degree, which can further facilitate the PL shifting from yellow to red. Owning to multicolor emissions and S doping, the prepared PL-tunable CDs present versatility in various applications including fluorescent films, light-emitting diodes (LED), bioimaging probe and highly sensitive Fe3+ detector.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tunable fluorescence
Sulfur doping
Water engineering
Light-emitting diodes
Intracellular Fe3+ detection

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of the PL-tunable CDs
Fluorescent mechanism of the PL-tunable CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-b) Optical property of prepared CDs after S doping and water engineering under different temperatures and times. (c) Fluorescent emission spectra of G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs in DMF under different excitation. (d) UV–vis absorption, PL emission, and PL excitation spectra of G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs in DMF. Fluorescent photographs are all taken under 365 nm UV light irradiation.
(a) TEM and HRTEM images (inset) of G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CD. (b) Raman spectra, (c) FT-IR spectra and (d) The full range XPS spectra of the three selected CDs.
High-resolution XPS C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra of (a) G-CDs, (b) Y-CDs and (c) R-CDs. Each band was deconvoluted following the literature.

(a) Fluorescent images of G-, Y- and R-CDs/PMMA composite films and R-CDs/PVA film under UV light. (b) Corresponding normalized PL spectra of above CDs/polymers composite films under excitation of 365 nm. (c) The photoluminescent photographs of the green, yellow, orange and red down-conversion LED devices by coating CDs/PMMA (green, yellow and red lights) and CDs/PVA (orange light) films on different chips. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescent emission spectra of Y-CDs upon addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 μM, respectively). (b) Relationship between F0/F and concentration of Fe3+, where F0 and F are fluorescent intensities of Y-CDs at 560 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. (c) Fluorescent intensity ratios (F/F0) of Y-CDs in the presence and absence of various metal ions at 560 nm. The fluorescent spectrum was measured under the excitation at 360 nm. (d) Confocal fluorescence microscopy of Y-CDs with different concentration of Fe3+ applied to MC3T3 cells under 405 nm laser excitation.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of PL-tunable CDs.
Schematic diagram of the possible (a) structure models and (b) energy level of these PL-tunable CDs.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence-tunable carbon dots from synergy effect of sulfur doping and water engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Gastroenterology, Sichuan Provincial People’s Hospital, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 610064, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Gastroenterology, Sichuan Provincial People’s Hospital, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 610064, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile solvothermal method is developed to synthesize green, yellow and red carbon dots by S doping and water engineering.

Multiple-color-emissive carbon dots (CDs) is one of the hotspots due to its potential in multi-fields. However, the exploring of efficient synthetic strategies and emission mechanisms still remain challenging. Herein, we report a facile solvothermal route to prepare CDs with tunable photoluminescence (PL) from green to red via a novel PL-tuning strategy of sulfur doping and water engineering. By detailed characterizations and comparison, it is demonstrated that the S doping can significantly increase the content of graphitic nitrogen, resulting in the fluorescence shifting from green to yellow; on the other hand, water engineering is able to yield carboxyl group and enhance surface oxidation degree, which can further facilitate the PL shifting from yellow to red. Owning to multicolor emissions and S doping, the prepared PL-tunable CDs present versatility in various applications including fluorescent films, light-emitting diodes (LED), bioimaging probe and highly sensitive Fe3+ detector.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging luminescent carbon material, have drawn extensive attention since the first discovery in 2004 [1]. Great efforts have been made to develop this novel luminescent material in the past decade, and so far, CDs characterized with superior merits in terms of tunable fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility and high photostability can be readily synthesized from thousands of low-cost precursors and hundreds of routes [2–5]. Based on these remarkable properties, CDs have been demonstrated to be potential candidates in various applications, such as bio-imaging, analyte detection and light emitting devices [6–11].

CDs with multicolor emissions, especially in long wavelength regime (e.g. red and near-infrared lights) have become a research hotspot and are highly desired since practical applications require efficient full-color emissive CDs [12,13]. For example, red emissive CDs possess deep tissue penetration for biological imaging [14], multicolor emissive CDs are indispensable components for white LEDs [15]. By far, a few reports concerning CDs with red fluorescence and tunable PL ranging from blue to red have been highlighted [16]. Among plenty of synthetic approaches, solvo/hydrothermal processes have shown great promise due to their convenience and controllability. However, most of the resulting multicolor CDs suffer from complicated, time-consuming and low-yield separation processes, such as silica column chromatography, anion-exchange column [17,18]. Accordingly, developing novel, facile and controllable solvothermal strategies to prepare multicolor emissive CDs is still highly desirable.

For facile and efficient PL-tuning strategies, a few reports have emphasized that the selected solvents can modulate the optical properties of prepared CDs through engineering both carbon core states and surface states [10,19]. Besides, heteroatom doping is regarded with high efficiency in PL-tuning. Apart from the most widely studied nitrogen (N) doping, whose different doping forms including amino, pyrrolic, pyridinic and graphitic have been demonstrated capable of adjusting PL properties of CDs [14,20], it is worth mentioning that sulfur (S) doping in the carbon framework can exert a significant impact on the properties of carbon-based materials, such as carbon microspheres, graphene and activated carbons [21,22]. However, although a few S-doped and N, S co-doped CDs with superior optical properties have been reported [23,24], little work has been done to probe the effect of S doping on the structure and optical properties of CDs. Hence, we believe that S doping and solvent engineering can facilitate the PL properties of prepared CDs and simultaneously support to comprehend the effect of S doping on the structure and optical properties of N, S co-doped CDs.

Herein, we report a novel, facile and controllable solvothermal strategy to prepare multicolor emissive CDs by S doping and solvent engineering. By adding thiourea and water, PL-tunable CDs from green to red are obtained (Scheme 1 ). Detailed characterizations reveal that S doping can significantly increase the amount of graphitic nitrogen while water engineering can efficiently yield carboxyl group and enhance surface oxidation degree. Besides, we reveal that both the graphitic N and carboxyl group can regulate the energy gap of prepared CDs, thus resulting in distinct PL behaviors. Finally, we demonstrate the versatility of as-synthesized PL-tunable CDs in the fields of fluorescent films, light-emitting diodes, biomedical imaging and detecting Fe3+ due to their multicolor emissions and S doping.

O-Phenylenediamine (oPD), thiourea and poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), trichloromethane, tetrahydrofuran, acetone, ethanol, methanol and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and poly (vinylalcohol) (PVA) were purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory, Chengdu. α-Modified Eagle Medium (α-MEM) came from Hyclone (USA). Methyl thiazolyltetrazolium (MTT) was purchased from Amreso, USA. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and utilized as obtained without further purification.

In a typical procedure for synthesis of G-CDs, 2.0 g of o-PD were dissolved in 40 mL of DMF. Then the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 5 h. After the reactor was cooled down to room temperature automatically, the obtained solution was purified via 0.22 μm filter to remove large particles. After removing solvent through rotary evaporator, the obtained products were dispersed in water and centrifuged (16,000 r min −1, 10 min) twice to wash off unreacted starting materials, and then freeze-dried to give G-CDs. For Y-CDs, the synthesis procedure was nearly the same as G-CDs, except that 0.5 g of thiourea was added. As for R-CDs, apart from adding 0.5 g of thiourea, the solvent was substituted by a mixed solution of 30 mL of DMF and 10 mL of ultrapure water.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) micrographs were recorded on FEI Tecnai F20 ransmission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrophotometer. XPS data was carried out on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer. Raman shift data was received using a LabRAM HR with λex = 784 nm. NMR was done on Bruker BioSpin AV11-600 MHz. UV–Vis absorption was characterized by a UV 3600. Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were collected using Horiba fluorescence spectrophotometer. Absolute quantum yield measurements were performed with a calibrated integrating sphere on Horiba spectrometer. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a Horiba corrected single photon-counting (TCSPC) system.

The cell viability of the three as-obtained CDs (i.e. G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs) against MC3T3 cells was assessed by MTT assay. Typically, MC3T3 cells were cultured into a 96-well cell culture plate with a density of 1 × 104 cells/well. After incubated at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 overnight, the culture medium was substituted with 300 μL of α-MEM containing different doses of as-prepared CDs (0, 25, 50, 75, 100 and 150 μg/mL, respectively) and the cells were incubated for 24 h again. After incubation, the culture medium was discarded and 20 μL of MTT with a concentration of 5.0 mg/mL was joined in every well. After incubation for another 4 h, the added MTT solution was discarded and 200 μL of DMSO was subsequently added to each well to dissolve purple formazan salts. Ultimatly, the absorbance of MTT was recorded using a multidetection microplate reader (Bio-Teklnstru-ments Inc., USA) at 490 nm. Notably, all experiments were performed in triplicate.

Leica TCS-SP5 laser scanning confocal microscope (CLSM) were used to measure the cellular fluorescence images of prepared CDs. Typically, approximately 1 × 105 MC3T3 cells were seeded in culture dishes (diameter: 35 mm dish with 14 mm bottom well) and incubated in 1 mL α-MEM at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 for 24 h. After the removal of culture medium, 1 mL of α-MEM medium containing 50 µg mL−1 of CDs was added for 4 h of incubation. Next, cultured MC3T3 cells were washed twice times utilizing phosphate buffered saline (PBS) to discard non-specifically bound CDs. Finally, the cells were recorded by CLSM using laser of 405 nm.

For the synthesis of green/yellow/red emissive films, 2 mL of the corresponding CDs/DMF solution (2.0 mg/mL) was added into 8 mL of PMMA/DMF solution (0.1 g/mL). After ultrasonic treatment, the mixed solution was then heated to a gel. Finally, the obtained gel was dropped casting on a cleaned culture dishes and dried under ambient circumstances.

For the orange emitting film, 2 mL of the R-CDs/ethanol solution (2.0 mg/mL) was mixed with 8 mL of PVA aqueous solution (0.1 g/mL). After ultrasonic treatment, the mixture was put in culture dishes and dried under ambient circumstances.

Multicolor emissive LED devices were fabricated using CDs/polymer fluorescent films and commercial LED chip. For the green LED, G-CDs mixture was used to form PMMA composite and drop-casted on the LED chip (395 nm). Similarly, Y-CDs/PMMA composite solution was casted on the LED chip (460 nm) for the yellow LED. R-CDs/PVA composite solution was casted on the LED chip (460 nm) for the orange LED. Finally, R-CDs/PMMA composite solution and LED chip (530 nm) were used for the red LED.

In a typical assay, different concentrations of Fe3+ were separately added into 2 mL of Y-CDs solution (1 mg/mL). After thoroughly mixed, the fluorescent intensity at 560 nm under excitation of 360 nm were measured after 5 min.

The selectivity of Y-CDs towards Fe3+ was evaluated by adding other common metal ions solutions (including Ca2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+) instead of Fe3+ and determined in the same way.

Fig. 1 a-b show the multicolor emissive CDs synthesized by S doping and water engineering. The addition of thiourea, which acts as sulfur source, faciliates the fluorescence of prepared CDs from green to yellow and orange lights (Figs. S1 and S2). After further engineering by water, red fluorescent CDs can be realized (Fig. S3). Just as expected, CDs with tunable fluorescence ranging from green to red were successfully fabricated by S doping and water engineering in a solvotherm approach, experimentally confirming the feasibility of our design. It should be noting that the solvothermal temperature exerts a great impact on the fluorescence shifting from green to yellow and red lights, which should be related to the high melting point of thiourea.

O-phenylenediamine (oPD) is widely utilized to synthesis CDs, but when using o-PD alone as precursor, most resulting CDs merely exhibit green emissions [18], which is further confirmed in this report. However, when o-PD serving as one of the main precursors, red emissive CDs can be obtained [25,26], suggesting that o-PD plays an important role in the generation of red PL. Herein, in order to investigate the role of o-PD, we conducted control experiments by solvothermal thiourea alone and all other conditions were kept constant. It can be clearly seen that the samples obtained by solvothermal sole thiourea show no fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) irradiation (Fig. S4). These results suggest that although o-PD alone can merely gain green emissive CDs, it is indispensable for the generation of yellow and red PL.

Actually, 220 ℃ and 5 h are found to be the optimal reaction conditions for the preparation of three distinct fluorescent CDs, accordingly, the three typical CDs featured by green, yellow, and red fluorescence are selected for further characterizations (Scheme. 1), aiming at exploring the effects of S doping and water engineering on the structure and optical properties of prepared CDs. Based on their emitting colors, they are denoted as G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the PL-tunable CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1c, the fluorescent spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs are featured by an excitation-independent behavior with peaks centered at 525 and 625 nm, respectively. For Y-CDs, the fluorescent spectrum shows a slight excitation-dependent emission with maximum emission peaks shifting from 550 nm to 575 nm as the excitation wavelength changes from 360 nm to 520 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the three selected CDs (Fig. 1d) exhibit analogous absorption in the UV region with a single peak located at around 350 nm, which is frequently attributed to the n–π* transition of conjugated CO and CN. While in the lower-energy region, the three selected CDs display distinct absorption bands at around 410 nm, 460 nm and 525 nm, respectively, demonstrating different surface states for these CDs. Moreover, the PL excitation (PLE) spectra of the three samples taken at optimal emission peaks for G-, Y- and R-CDs at 525 nm, 575 nm and 625 nm are found to be well overlapped with corresponding absorption bands in the lower-energy region (Fig. 1d), indicating that the green, yellow and red-light emissions of the three chosen CDs originate from the transition of absorption bands in the lower-energy region. The absolute quantum yields (QYs) for G-, Y- and R-CDs are determined to be 22%, 26% and 20% (Fig. S5), respectively, using an integrating sphere under their optimal excitation wavelengths, which are comparable to previous reports [17,18].

The sizes and morphologies of the three typical CDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the three kinds of CDs are well-dispersed and the average diameters are determined to be 8.45 nm, 8.79 nm and 9.96 nm for G-, Y- and R-CDs, respectively, suggesting their similarity in particle size. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images display that the three CDs exhibit similar well­resolved lattice fringes with a distance of 0.21 nm, which should be attributed to the (1 0 0) in-plane lattice of graphene [25]. It is worth noting that the particle size distributions of the three CDs are broad, which range from 5 to 13 nm, 5 to 12 nm and 7 to 13 nm for G-, Y- and R-CDs (Fig. S6), respectively. These results suggest that the quantum size effect should not be responsible for the differences in fluorescence. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra (1H and 13C) were characterized to discriminate the sp3-hybridized carbon atoms from the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. In the 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S7a), sp2 carbons are discovered. Signals from aromatic H are observed in the range of 6.5–8.5 ppm for the three CDs, which are generally ascribed to graphitized cores’ proton resonances [27]. In the 13C NMR spectra (Fig. S7b), signals in the scope of 8–100 ppm generally correspond to the aliphatic (sp3) carbon atoms, while signals located in 100–185 ppm represent aromatic (sp2) carbon atoms [28], indicating that the three CDs all consist of graphitic structures and amorphous structures. The Raman spectra of the three CDs (Fig. 2b) further substantiate the above results of NMR spectra. Two typical peaks at around 1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 are found for all the three CDs and refer to the disordered surfaces (D band) and graphitic networks (G band) in their frameworks, respectively. It is well-known that the ratio of ID/IG generally stands for the graphite level. Here, the intensity ratios of ID/IG are determined to be 0.92, 1.23 and 1.09 for G-, Y- and R-CDs, which are irregular and inconsistent with the red-shift of fluorescence from G-CDs to R-CDs, signifying that the extent of graphitization should not be the crucial parameter responsible for the shift of PL from green to red.

Fourier transform infrared (FT­IR) spectra and X­ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were employed to study the possible surface states and compositions resulting in the conversion in PL properties. The FT-IR spectra displayed in Fig. 2c reveal that all the three CDs possess abundant functional groups such as OH/NH (3000–3500 cm−1), CO (1678 cm−1), CO (1130 cm−1) on their surface, thus guaranteeing their good solubility in various solvents. Moreover, the stretching vibration of CC (1586 cm−1), CN (1620 cm−1), CN (1360 cm−1) are also detected in the three CDs, indicating the formation of polyaromatic structures in their frameworks. Interestingly, the stretching vibration intensity of CN (1620 cm−1) and CN (1360 cm−1) for G-CDs are much lower than those of Y-CDs and R-CDs, indicating that G-CDs possess a lower amount of graphitic N in comparison with Y-CDs and R-CDs. Furthermore, a new peak appears at around 1770 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs, which generally corresponds to carboxyl group. Besides, two strong peaks positioned at 2059 cm−1 and 618 cm−1 are pronounced in both Y-CDs and R-CDs, assigning to SCN and CS groups, respectively, demonstrating the successful doping of S element in Y-CDs and R-CDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was further employed to investigate the compositions and surfaces of these CDs. The full range XPS spectra presented in Fig. 2d show that the three CDs all contain C, N, and O elements while Y- and R-CDs additionally possess S element. The content of each element is shown in Table S1. Notably, the decrease of C content from G-CDs to Y-CDs is very approximative to the amount of S element in Y-CDs, which manifest that the doped S element in Y-CDs mainly substitutes the origin C element. In addition, a drop in S content from 8.2% to 5.8% and a significant rise in O content from 3.2% to 7.1% are observed for Y-CDs and R-CDs, suggesting that water engineering can efficiently enhance the degree of surface oxidation for R-CDs. The high-resolution C1s spectra show irregular CC/CC (≈284.4 eV) content with the increasing emission wavelength from G-CDs to Y-CDs (Fig. 3 and Table S2). The inclination is in consistent with Raman spectra data, further implying no relationship between graphite level and PL red-shift. Carboxyl carbon at 288.2 eV is observed in R-CDs alone, as confirmed by FT-IR spectra. The high-resolution N1s spectra of the three CDs show two pronounced peaks for pyridinic N and graphitic N. Notably, nitrogen stemming from pyridinic N dominates in G-CDs, whereas graphitic N is the prevailing structure unit in Y-CDs and R-CDs, which is in full accordance with FT-IR data. In the high-resolution O1s spectra, R-CDs have the highest amount of CO group, indirectly proving the generation of carboxyl group and enhancement of oxidation degree. The high-resolution S2p spectra of Y-CDs and R-CDs are presented in Fig. S8. Similar peak for C-S-C covalent bond is observed in both Y-CDs and R-CDs, while another peak at higher energy (167.8 eV) is observed in R-CDs, corresponding to CSOx sulfone bridges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent mechanism of the PL-tunable CDs
ce:section.ce:para: So far, the PL mechanisms of CDs are generally elucidated by two well-known models, which are carbon core state and surface state, respectively. In our case, TEM, HR-TEM, Raman spectra high-resolution C1s spectra confirm that the three CDs have very approximative particle size, broad size distribution, well­resolved lattice spacing and irregular graphitization level, hence, the red-shift of PL from G-CDs to R-CDs is independent of particle size or sp2­conjugated domains. FT-IR and XPS data further reveal that pyridinic N dominates in G-CDs, which has been demonstrated capable of emitting green light [29]. In addition, the content of graphitic nitrogen of Y-CDs and R-CDs is far more than that of G-CDs, which is determined to be 74.63% for Y-CDs, 59.55% for R-CDs and 5.07% for G-CDs (Table S3), respectively. Such a huge difference implies that the graphitic N is one of the most significant factors related to the transformation of PL properties. It has been proved both theoretically and experimentally that the graphitic N is an electron donating element and capable of creating midgap states in the original gap, which can efficiently narrow the band gap of prepared CDs, resulting in red-shift of fluorescence [30,31]. Consequently, Y- CDs and R-CDs with large amount of graphitic N display red-shift emissions in comparison with G-CDs. However, R-CDs, which have the maximum emission wavelength among the three CDs, conversely have relative lower content of graphitic nitrogen (59.55%) compared with Y-CDs (74.63%), indicating that the red-shift of fluorescence from yellow to red is caused by another factor instead of graphitic N. Notably, R-CDs have higher oxygen content and oxidation degree, and moreover, give rise to new carboxyl group on the surface. According to the density functional theory of Chen et al. [32], carboxyl group attached on sp2-hybridized carbons can cause remarkable local distortions, thus narrowing the energy gap, leading to the red-shift of fluorescence, Therefore, in this report, the red-shift of PL from Y-CDs to R-CDs should be ascribed to surface carboxyl group.

On the basis of above results and conclusions, we propose the possible structure models and energy level to explain the red-shift of fluorescence from G-CDs to R-CDs. As shown in Scheme 2 a, it is clear that the green emission of G-CDs comes from pyridinic N while yellow fluorescence of Y-CDs originates from the increased amount of graphitic nitrogen. The increasement of graphitic N can efficiently create midgap states, narrowing the band gap, thus resulting in red-shift of emission band from green to yellow. Subsequently, water engineering increases the degree of surface oxidation and generates carboxyl group, further narrowing the band gap, leading to a red-shift of fluorescence from yellow to red. In a word, the yellow fluorescence originates from the increased graphitic N while the red fluorescence is contributed from the synergy effect of the graphitic N and carboxyl group. A possible band structure is also proposed to explain the photoluminescence process of the as-prepared CDs with multiple emission states. PLE spectra indicate that the PL is contributed from two excitation bands. The excitation band in the range of 350–380 nm should be attributed to n → π* (HOMO → LUMO) transition of CO/CN bonds. The excitation band at around 400 nm (3.10 eV), 480 nm (2.58 eV) and 530 nm (2.34 eV) may be ascribed to the transition of N–/O-related emission stats. Therefore, as shown in Scheme 2b, there are N-related emission states and O-related emission states for these CDs, representing different electron transition processes. After irradiation, electron in HOMO can be excited to LUMO. Subsequently, most of these excited electrons are transferred into the N-related states and O-related states, and then multicolor PL of green, yellow and red can be emitted by recombination between electrons in the N/O state and holes in the HOMO. Specifically, G-CDs are dominated by pyridinic N, the N-related emission sate formed by pyridinic N emits green light (λ = 525 nm, 2.36 eV). In the case of Y-CDs, the amount of graphitic N significantly increases and becomes prominent after S doping, thus generating a new N-related state, which can emit yellow light (575 nm, 2.15 eV). With respect to R-CDs, apart from the N-related state formed by graphitic N, water engineering further gives rise to carboxyl group on the surface, resulting in O-related state. Red light (λ = 625 nm, 1.98 eV) can be emitted from the irradiation center formed by carboxyl group.

Apart from the proposed PL mechanisms, the fact that S doping can increase the amount of graphitic N seems interesting, but the process may be confusing and hard to comprehend. We have demonstrated that solvothermal treatment of o-PD alone can merely gain green emissive CDs while thiourea alone cannot form fluorescent CDs (Figs. S1 and S4). According to these results, we can deduce that during the formation process of CDs, o-PD may serve as the main part to form the initial green emissive CDs, while the added thiourea can provide additional N and S elements to participate in decorating the initial product, resulting in the transformation of internal structure and surface group. Graphitic N is one of various changes, and due to its dramatic increase and ability to affect the energy gap of CDs, we hence believe that it is graphitic N that induce the red-shift of fluorescence from green to yellow. To the best of our knowledge, the formation process of CDs and the role of precursors have not been well investigated so far and still remain big challenge [33]. Therefore, the concrete process that S doping affect the graphitic nitrogen composition of prepared CDs needs further intensive exploration.

Benefiting from the superior optical performances, the PL-tunable CDs exhibit potential in various applications. Firstly, based on the abundant functional groups on the surface, the three selected CDs all possess excellent solubility in various solvents and display multicolor emissions (Figs. S9–11). In this case, we fabricate multicolor emitting films consisting of CDs and polymers. Herein, poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) is selected as the matrix owing to its good solubility in DMF [34], which can yield green-, yellow- and red-light emitting films. On the other hand, due to the solvent- dependent emission of R-CDs, alcohol polymer poly (vinylalcohol) (PVA) is chosen as matrix to prepare orange-light emitting films (Fig. S11) [35]. As shown in Fig. 4 a–b, the as-prepared G-, Y- and R-CDs/PMMA films emit bright green, yellow and red fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) irradiation, which are in well agreement with that of original CDs. Furthermore, the R-CDs/PVA film expectantly exhibits orange emission under irradiation of 365 nm UV light, corresponding to the emitting light of R-CDs/ethanol solution, implying that the synthesized PL-tunable CDs can maintain their original fluorescence in the solid state. Therefore, the above synthesized PL-tunable composite films were further applied to encapsulate LED chips. As shown in Fig. 4c, color-tunable LED devices containing green, yellow, orange and red lights by coating above CDs/polymer composite films on different chips (chip emission at 395, 460, 460 and 530 nm for green, yellow, orange and red LED, respectively, corresponding to their optimal excitation wavelength) are successfully fabricated. These results further reveal that the as-prepared color-tunable CDs are promising candidate in the field of lighting devices.

Since CDs have demonstrated possessing potentiality in bioimaging due to their superior optical properties and excellent biocompatibility [26,36,37], accordingly, the three selected CDs are expected to potentially serve as bioimaging reagents. Standard MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assays towards MC3T3 cells were conducted to evaluate the cytotoxicity of these CDs. As shown in Fig. S12, over 90% of MC3T3 cells are viable after incubation of 24 h with CDs concentrations ranging from 25 to 150 mg/mL, indicating that the three CDs are almost nontoxic. Subsequently, the cell imaging capabilities of the three CDs were preliminarily investigated. After incubating MC3T3 cells with the three CDs for 4 h, the MC3T3 cells successfully uptake these CDs and emit bright yellow fluorescence for G-CDs and Y-CDs, orange fluorescence for R-CDs (Fig. S13), respectively, under a confocal microscope with 405 nm laser excitation. These results imply that these CDs possess good prospect to serve as fluorescent probe for biological applications. It should be note that G-CDs and R-CDs display unexpected yellow and orange lights after dispersed in water. For G-CDs, the red-shift of PL from green to yellow in water may be ascribed to the high polarity of water [38]. R-CDs show solvent-dependent emission ranging from yellow to red as the solvent polarity increases. But in aqueous solution, R-CDs only emit orange light, this may be due to the relatively poor solubility in water.

Fe3+, as one of the indispensable elements in biological systems, plays crucial roles in many biological processes [39], therefore, approaches allowed to visually detect Fe3+ are of great significance and desire further investigation [40]. On the other hand, N, S-doped CDs have been frequently reported possessing excellent sensing performance towards Fe3+ [41]. In this case, Y-CDs, which is co-doped with N and S element and meanwhile has the highest QYs among the three CDs, is selected to explore the feasibility serving as fluorescent probe to detect Fe3+. After adding Fe3+ with concentrations ranging from 0 to 500 μM, the PL intensity of Y-CDs at 560 nm gradually decreases (Fig. 5 a). Moreover, the intensity ratios (F0/F) of Fe3+-Y-CDs display a good linear relationship with the concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 0–500 μM and the relationship can be well fitted into the equation of (F0/F) = 0.003[Fe3+] + 0.9812 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9963 (Fig. 5b), where F0 and F are fluorescent intensities of Y-CDs at 560 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. The detection limit is calculated to be 120 nM, suggesting that as-prepared Y-CDs exhibit relative high sensitivity for Fe3+ detection. Besides, fluorescent intensity ratios (F/F0) of Y-CDs in the presence and absence of various metal ions including Fe3+, Ca2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+ were recorded to further estimate the selectivity of Y-CDs towards various metal ions. It clearly shows that Fe3+ displays greatest quenching effect on the fluorescence of Y-CDs among these metal ions (Fig. 5c and Fig. S14), indicating that Y-CDs possess high selectivity for Fe3+ detecting. In this case, we further investigated the feasibility and applicability for monitoring intracellular Fe3+ levels since intracellular environment is extremely complex. It is observed that upon supplementing cells with incremental concentrations of Fe3+, as expected, the intensity of fluorescence from MC3T3 cells gradually decreases (Fig. 5d). This result implies that Y-CDs not only possess high sensitivity and selectively for Fe3+ detecting in real complex environments, but also can serve as an effective probe for detecting intracellular Fe3+ with yellow fluorescence emission.

To data, the mechanisms of CDs as Fe3+ probe have been extensively investigated and frequently attributed to that Fe3+ can easily coordinate with surface functional groups of prepared CDs to form Fe3+-complexes. The formation of Fe3+-complexes on the surface could change the surface electron–hole recombination annihilation and finally lead to PL quenching [39–42]. Herein, in order to investigate the PL quenching process of Y-CDs in the presence of Fe3+, fluorescence lifetime decay was measured. As shown in Fig. S15, the average fluorescence lifetime of Y-CDs (black line) is 6.9 ns. After the addition of Fe3+, the lifetime of Y-CDs-Fe3+ decreases to 2.8 ns (the red line). The significantly reduced fluorescence lifetime indicates a dynamic quenching occurred between the surface of Y-CDs and Fe3+, further confirming a fast electron transfer process in the Y-CDs-Fe3+ system [43].

In summary, we have developed a novel, facile and controllable solvothermal approach for the preparation of multicolor tunable emissive CDs from green to red by a new PL-tuning strategy of S doping and solvent engineering using water. We demonstrate that S doping can significantly increase the amount of graphitic N while water engineering can enhance the oxidation degree and give rise to carboxyl group, both of which are able to efficiently narrow the band gap of prepared CDs, leading to the red-shift of fluorescence. Hence, yellow fluorescence originates from the increased amount of graphitic nitrogen while the red fluorescence should be ascribed to the synergy effect of graphitic N and carboxyl group. The as-prepared CDs display good solubility in various solvents and we further demonstrate their application of fabricating color-tunable CDs/polymers composite films. Subsequently, we show that these multicolor composite films can be fabricated as efficient LED devices with different color light emissions ranging from green to red. Besides, due to the low cytotoxicity and superior optical property in water, our CDs can also serve as fluorescent probe for bioimaging. Finally, benefitting from S doping, Y-CDs also exhibit high selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ detection. This work preliminarily demonstrates the effects of S doping and solvent engineering of water on the structure and optical properties of prepared CDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51603030 and 51673128).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.124199.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
